USA: Enron's bankruptcy filing could trigger SEC probe.
By Peter Eavis

10/26/2001
Reuters English News Service
(C) Reuters Limited 2001.

NEW YORK, Oct 26 (Reuters) - Enron Corp's bankruptcy filing could trigger an SEC probe into its dealings with partnerships run by its former chief financial officer, a bankruptcy lawyer said on Monday. 
"The SEC is going to want to know what the relationship was between the partnerships and Enron," said attorney Robert B. Badeer, who represents Enron's creditors in the bankruptcy case. 
The SEC could also look into whether Enron's board of directors breached its fiduciary duties to shareholders by allowing Andrew Fastow, who headed the partnerships, to run the company, Badeer said. 
Enron's board has said it was unaware of Fastow's involvement in the partnerships until recently. 
"The board has a fiduciary duty to shareholders to make sure that the company is run in a way that is in the best interests of the company," Badeer said.

USA: Enron's bankruptcy filing could trigger a wave of buybacks.
By Peter Eavis

10/26/2001
Reuters English News Service
(C) Reuters Limited 2001.

NEW YORK, Oct 26 (Reuters) - Enron Corp's bankruptcy filing could trigger a wave of buybacks by energy companies, analysts said on Monday. 
"It's a possibility that we're looking at," said John Kinser, an analyst at UBS Warburg in New York. 
"It's not a certainty, but it's a possibility." 
Enron, the largest energy trader, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection on Monday, saying it had $1.2 billion in assets and $1.9 billion in debts. 
The company's shares fell $1.50 to $13.50 in trading on the New York Stock Exchange. 
The company's shares have fallen more than 80 percent this year.

USA: Enron's bankruptcy filing could trigger a wave of buybacks.
By Peter Eavis

10/26/2001
Reuters English News Service
(C) Reuters Limited 2001.

NEW YORK, Oct 26 (Reuters) - Enron Corp's bankruptcy filing could trigger a wave of buybacks by energy companies, analysts said on Monday. 
"It's a possibility that we're looking at," said John Kinser, an analyst at UBS Warburg in New York. 
"It's not a certainty, but it's a possibility." 
Enron, the largest energy trader, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection on Monday, saying it had $1.2 billion in assets and $1.9 billion in debts. 
The company's shares fell $1.50 to $13.50 in trading on the New York Stock Exchange. 
The company's shares have fallen more than 80 percent this year.
